comments.body: Does this answer your question? Scanner is skipping nextLine() after using next() or nextFoo()?

When posting a question, make sure you methodically list each step required to reproduce your problem, include the exact text of your error message and indicate which line of code the error message complains about. You can't assume people are going to run your code to see what the problem is -- in fact, most people who provide answers read the code without running it.

answers.body:
You are missing the actual operation of adding removing from the list. @DrMe go through these program you can find where you'r doing wrong. I hope this can help you

import java.util.ArrayList;
import java.util.List;
import java.util.Scanner;

public class TestToDoList {

    private static List<String> currentList = new ArrayList<String>();

    public static void main(String[] args) {

        int menuItem = -1;
        while (menuItem != 0) {
            menuItem = menu();
            switch (menuItem) {
            case 1:
                showList();
                break;
            case 2:
                addItem();
                break;
            case 3:
                removeItem();
                break;
            case 0:
                break;
            default:
                System.out.println("Enter a valid option");
            }
        }
    }

    public static int menu() {
        System.out.println();
        System.out.println("----------------------");
        System.out.println("Main Menu");
        System.out.println("----------------------");
        System.out.println("0. Exit the program");
        System.out.println("1. Display to-do list");
        System.out.println("2. Add item to list");
        System.out.println("3. Remove item from list");
        System.out.println();
        System.out.print("Enter choice: ");
        int choice = scanner.nextInt();
        return choice;
    }

    public static void showList() {
        System.out.println();
        System.out.println("----------------------");       
        System.out.println("To-Do List");
        System.out.println("----------------------");
        int number = 0;
        for (String item : currentList) {
            System.out.println(++number + " " + item);
        }
        System.out.println("----------------------");


    }

    public static void addItem() {
        System.out.println("Add Item");
        System.out.println("----------------------");
        System.out.print("Enter an item: ");
        Scanner scanner = new Scanner(System.in);
        String item = scanner.nextLine();
        currentList.add(item);
        showList();
    }

    public static void removeItem() {
        System.out.println("Remove Item");
        System.out.println("----------------------");
        Scanner scanner = new Scanner(System.in);
        System.out.print("What do you want to remove?");
        int index = scanner.nextInt();
        if((index-1)<0 || index>currentList.size()) {
            System.out.println("Wrong index number! Please enter in range of 1 to "+currentList.size());            
        }else {
            currentList.remove(index-1);
        }
        System.out.println("----------------------");
        showList();


    }
}

Further improve using generic and utils methods.

comments.body: I believe the question that you answered is a duplicate and therefore should have been closed. Also, learn to use markdown to format your posts. I think that the comment (in the code in your answer), i.e. Consume newline character, does not properly explain why the call to method nextLine() is needed. I recommend that you take the tour and visit the help center.

answers.body:
import java.util.ArrayList;
import java.util.Scanner;

public class TodoList {
    private static ArrayList<String> tasks = new ArrayList<>();

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner scanner = new Scanner(System.in);
        boolean exit = false;
        while (!exit) {
            System.out.println("\n1. Add Task");
            System.out.println("2. View Tasks");
            System.out.println("3. Remove Task");
            System.out.println("4. Exit");
            System.out.print("Choose an option: ");
            int choice = scanner.nextInt();
            scanner.nextLine(); // Consume newline character

            switch (choice) {
                case 1:
                    addTask(scanner);
                    break;
                case 2:
                    viewTasks();
                    break;
                case 3:
                    removeTask(scanner);
                    break;
                case 4:
                    exit = true;
                    break;
                default:
                    System.out.println("Invalid choice. Please try again.");
            }
        }
    }

    private static void addTask(Scanner scanner) {
        System.out.print("Enter task: ");
        String task = scanner.nextLine();
        tasks.add(task);
        System.out.println("Task added successfully.");
    }

    private static void viewTasks() {
        if (tasks.isEmpty()) {
            System.out.println("No tasks available.");
        } else {
            System.out.println("Tasks:");
            for (int i = 0; i < tasks.size(); i++) {
                System.out.println((i + 1) + ". " + tasks.get(i));
            }
        }
    }

    private static void removeTask(Scanner scanner) {
        if (tasks.isEmpty()) {
            System.out.println("No tasks available to remove.");
        } else {
            viewTasks();
            System.out.print("Enter task number to remove: ");
            int index = scanner.nextInt();
            if (index > 0 && index <= tasks.size()) {
                tasks.remove(index - 1);
                System.out.println("Task removed successfully.");
            } else {
                System.out.println("Invalid task number.");
            }
        }
    }
}

Modern Java

We can tighten up, and simplify, your code using facilities in modern Java. Read on.

Scanner

The Scanner class is made for helping parse certain inputs, not specifically for console interactions.

I suggest avoiding Scanner for your purpose. Just parse the string input yourself.

String input = "42";
try
{
    int number = Integer.parseInt( input );
    …
}
catch ( NumberFormatException e ) 
{
    … Handle faulty input … 
}

IO

Java 25 bring the IO class as a convenient alternative to System.out & System.in.

It offers print, println, and readln methods. That last one, readln, has the nice feature of allowing you to pass in the user prompt (for less lines of code overall).

switch without break

The switch statement has been improved to now offer Arrow labels syntax. This simplifies the code, and eliminates the need for break in each case.

Text blocks

Text blocks make working with multi-line text easier.

Example code

There are many ways to accomplish your goal of adding strings to a list. Here in this example we pass a reference to the list on to each of the menu item handlers. The idea is to avoid spaghetti code with global variables. Instead of having the handler methods like addTask reach outside itself for the task list, we pass into the method what it needs (a reference to a list).

And, actually, we pass the reference as the more general Collection to give our app the opportunity to change the implementation of the tasks collection to something other than a List. It's usually best to use the most general of interfaces or superclasses that still has the needed functionality. In addTask method, the only functionality we need is the Collection#add method.

This example implements only one of the four menu handlers, leaving the rest as an exercise for the reader.

package work.basil.example;

import java.util.ArrayList;
import java.util.List;

public class ConsoleToDoList
{
    static void main ( )
    {
        ConsoleToDoList app = new ConsoleToDoList();
        app.runUserInterface();
    }

    private void runUserInterface ( )
    {
        final List < String > tasks = new ArrayList <>();
        final int MIN = 1, MAX = 5;
        int choice = - 1;
        while ( choice < MIN || choice > MAX )
        {
            IO.println( this.menu() );
            String input = IO.readln( "Enter menu number: " );
            try
            {
                choice = Integer.parseInt( input );
                switch ( choice )
                {
                    case 1 -> this.addTask( tasks );
                    case 2 -> IO.println( "Delete a task" );
                    case 3 -> IO.println( "Show the list of tasks" );
                    case 4 -> IO.println( "Delete all tasks" );
                    case 5 ->
                    {
                        IO.println( "Goodbye." );
                        return;
                    }
                    default -> IO.println( "Invalid choice." );
                }
            }
            catch ( NumberFormatException e )
            {
                IO.println( "Invalid input. Type a number between " + MIN + " and " + MAX + "." );
            }
            choice = - 1; // Reset for next loop.
        }
    }

    private void addTask ( final Collection < String > theTasks )
    {
        String input = "";
        while ( input.isBlank() )
        {
            input = IO.readln( "Enter task name: " ).trim().strip();
        }
        theTasks.add( input );
        IO.println( "Task added." );
    }

    private String menu ( )
    {
        return """
                
                ==== Menu ================
                1. Add a task
                2. Delete a task
                3. Show the list of tasks
                4. Delete all tasks
                5. Quit this program
                """;
    }
}

Example run:


==== Menu ================
1. Add a task
2. Delete a task
3. Show the list of tasks
4. Delete all tasks
5. Quit this program

Enter menu number: 2
Delete a task

==== Menu ================
1. Add a task
2. Delete a task
3. Show the list of tasks
4. Delete all tasks
5. Quit this program

Enter menu number: 3
Show the list of tasks

==== Menu ================
1. Add a task
2. Delete a task
3. Show the list of tasks
4. Delete all tasks
5. Quit this program

Enter menu number: 1
Enter task name: Chop wood
Task added.

==== Menu ================
1. Add a task
2. Delete a task
3. Show the list of tasks
4. Delete all tasks
5. Quit this program

Enter menu number: 5
Goodbye.

Process finished with exit code 0

title: How to make a To-Do List in java?
body:
I need to create a functioning To-Do list in java that allows a user to:

  1. Add an item

  2. Delete an item

  3. Show the list

  4. Delete all tasks

  5. Exit the program

I am currently having trouble adding an item to my list. Each time I enter an item to add this is the output I receive: 

Exception in thread "main" java.util.InputMismatchException
at java.base/java.util.Scanner.throwFor(Scanner.java:939)
at java.base/java.util.Scanner.next(Scanner.java:1594)
at java.base/java.util.Scanner.nextInt(Scanner.java:2258)
at java.base/java.util.Scanner.nextInt(Scanner.java:2212)
at Test.menu(Test.java:50)
at Test.main(Test.java:11)

Here is what I have tried so far, any help would be greatly appreciated: 

import java.util.Scanner;
import java.util.ArrayList;

public class Test {

public static void main(String[] args) {

    int menuItem = -1;

    while(menuItem !=0) {
        menuItem = menu();

        switch(menuItem) {

        case 1: 
            showList();
            break;

        case 2: 
            addItem();
            break;

        case 3: 
            removeItem();
            break;

        case 0: 
            break;

        default: 
            System.out.println("Enter a valid option");
        }

    }
}

public static int menu() {

int choice; 

Scanner keyboard = new Scanner(System.in);
System.out.println("Main Menu");
System.out.println();
System.out.println("0. Exit the program");
System.out.println("1. Display to-do list");
System.out.println("2. Add item to list");
System.out.println("3. Remove item from list");
System.out.println();
System.out.print("Enter choice: ");
choice = keyboard.nextInt();

return choice;
}

public static void showList() {

System.out.println("To-Do List");

Scanner input = new Scanner(System.in);
String line;
int number = 1;

while (input.hasNextLine()){
    line = input.nextLine();
    System.out.println(number + " ");
    System.out.println(line);
    ++number;
}

System.out.println();


}

public static void addItem() {

System.out.println("Add Item");

Scanner input = new Scanner(System.in);
System.out.println("Enter an item: ");
String item = input.nextLine();
System.out.println(item);


}

public static void removeItem() {

int choice;
showList();

Scanner input = new Scanner(System.in);
System.out.println("What do you want to remove?");
choice = input.nextInt();

ArrayList<String> items = new ArrayList<String>();
int number = 1;

Scanner input2 = new Scanner(System.in);
String item; 

while (input2.hasNextLine()) {
    item = input2.nextLine();

    if (number != choice) 
        items.add(item);

    ++number;   
}

for(int i = 0; i < items.size(); i++)
    System.out.println(items.get(i));

    }
}
